content.problemStatement:
Given a binary tree, check whether it is a structural mirror of itself (i.e., structurally symmetric around its center).

For example, the following binary tree is structurally symmetric:




Input Format

The first line of the input contains a single integer NNN — the number of nodes in the binary tree.
Next N−1N - 1N−1 lines contain three space separated characters pi,ci,Rip_i, c_i, R_ipi​,ci​,Ri​, describing the tree with edge informations - pip_ipi​ is an integer denoting the parent node of the iiith edge, cic_ici​ is child node, and the letter RiR_iRi​ denotes whether the child is left child or right child, it's value is `L` if cic_ici​ is left child of pip_ipi​, else `R`.


Output Format
Output on the single line, `YES` if the given binary tree is structurally symmetric to itself, `NO` otherwise.
constraints: All pi and ci values are distinct, forming a proper binary tree
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: The first line contains an integer N, the number of nodes in the binary tree (1 ≤ N ≤ 10000). The next N-1 lines each contain three space-separated values: pi ci Ri, where pi is the parent node index, ci is the child node index, and Ri is either 'L' (ci is the left child of pi) or 'R' (ci is the right child of pi). All node indices are between 1 and N and are distinct. The tree is guaranteed to be a valid binary tree.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output 'YES' if the given binary tree is structurally symmetric, otherwise output 'NO'.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The tree is symmetric: left subtree of node 1 (nodes 2,6,7) mirrors the right subtree (nodes 3,4,5).
Node 2 has a left child while node 3 has only a right child, breaking mirror symmetry.
Both left and right subtrees consist of a single leaf, so the tree is symmetric.

solution.approach: To determine if a binary tree is structurally symmetric, we can compare the left subtree with the mirrored right subtree. This can be achieved by using a recursive function that checks if two trees are mirror images of each other. The function should return true if both trees are null, or if they are not null and the left child of the first tree is a mirror of the right child of the second tree, and the right child of the first tree is a mirror of the left child of the second tree.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, O(T*N) total, where N is the number of nodes in the tree and T is the number of test cases.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for the recursive call stack in the worst case (when the tree is skewed).
